primary_diagnosis.name: Biliary colic due to symptomatic cholelithiasis

pathophysiology_summary: A gallstone transiently impacts the cystic duct (or gallbladder neck), and the gallbladder contracts against the obstruction — powerfully stimulated by cholecystokinin released after a fatty meal. The resulting distension of a viscus produces severe, deep, poorly-localised visceral pain referred to the right upper quadrant and, via shared T5-T9 / phrenic-nerve pathways, to the right shoulder/scapula. Because the pain is visceral and colicky, the patient is characteristically restless and cannot find comfort (in contrast to the stillness of peritoneal irritation). When the stone falls back into the gallbladder the obstruction relieves and the pain settles over 4-6 hours, WITHOUT the gallbladder-wall inflammation, fever or leucocytosis that define acute cholecystitis, and without the bile-duct obstruction, jaundice and sepsis of ascending cholangitis. The clinical task is to confirm the episode is uncomplicated (afebrile, anicteric, negative Murphy's, no peritonism) and safety-net for the red flags that signal progression.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe postprandial (fatty-meal) RUQ pain radiating to the right shoulder blade with restlessness and vomiting is the classic constellation.
Same territory as biliary colic; distinguished by fever, prolonged pain (>4-6h), positive Murphy's sign, guarding and leucocytosis — must be actively screened out.
Charcot's triad (RUQ pain + fever/rigors + jaundice) would signal a bile-duct obstruction with infection — a surgical/medical emergency; screened out here by absent fever and absent jaundice.
Epigastric/upper-abdominal postprandial pain can mimic biliary pain; less consistent with strict right-shoulder-blade radiation and the fatty-meal trigger.

differential.diagnosis: Acute myocardial infarction / acute coronary syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Upper-abdominal pain with diaphoresis can be an atypical cardiac presentation and must be considered; here the pain is right-sided radiating to the right scapula (not retrosternal/left-arm/jaw), which lowers but does not fully exclude it remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI should hold biliary colic as leading but keep cholecystitis, cholangitis and an atypical cardiac cause on the list until red-flag screening is done.

differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, no rigors, anicteric, pain now plateauing after ~3h — the picture of an uncomplicated, self-limiting biliary colic episode.

Cannot be fully excluded via telehealth — Murphy's sign, wall thickening on ultrasound and inflammatory markers require in-person assessment; the absence of fever makes it less likely but not impossible.
No fever, no rigors and no jaundice — Charcot's triad not met; remains a safety-net escalation target if any of these develop.

differential.diagnosis: Acute myocardial infarction / acute coronary syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Right-sided scapular radiation and a clear fatty-meal trigger make a cardiac cause less likely, but definitive exclusion needs an ECG/troponin that telehealth cannot provide.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Post-screen, biliary colic is clearly leading, but the correct disposition still hinges on the fact that cholecystitis and a cardiac cause cannot be excluded remotely — hence in-person assessment, not remote reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: Uncomplicated biliary colic (symptomatic cholelithiasis) — requires imaging to confirm and to exclude cholecystitis
differential.evidence_basis: Classic uncomplicated biliary colic; ultrasound expected to show mobile echogenic gallstones with acoustic shadowing and NO gallbladder-wall thickening. Diagnosis is presumptive until imaging and, where indicated, bloods are done.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor is not expected to confirm the diagnosis remotely; it is expected to reach a presumptive impression, arrange same-day in-person assessment + imaging, and safety-net.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever/rigors would shift the picture from uncomplicated biliary colic to acute cholecystitis or ascending cholangitis — infection of the gallbladder or biliary tree.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected cholecystitis/cholangitis can progress to gallbladder empyema, gangrene/perforation or biliary sepsis and death; missing it also risks false reassurance and home management of a surgical emergency.

red_flags.description: Jaundice (yellowing of skin/sclerae), dark urine or pale stools
red_flags.clinical_significance: Jaundice indicates common-bile-duct obstruction (choledocholithiasis) and, with fever, ascending cholangitis — a time-critical emergency needing urgent ERCP/decompression.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cholangitis causes overwhelming biliary sepsis; a missed obstructing CBD stone risks acute pancreatitis.

red_flags.description: Pain persisting continuously for more than 6 hours, or steadily worsening rather than settling
red_flags.clinical_significance: Biliary colic is self-limiting (typically <6h); pain that does not settle suggests the stone is still impacted and inflammation is developing — evolving acute cholecystitis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of cholecystitis increases the risk of gangrene/perforation and the need for emergency rather than elective surgery.

red_flags.description: Peritonism (rebound tenderness / involuntary guarding), or signs of sepsis or shock (high fever with low blood pressure, confusion, cold clammy collapse)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Peritonism and systemic instability signal perforation or biliary sepsis; these are examination/physiological findings that a telehealth encounter cannot assess directly.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Perforation and septic shock are rapidly fatal without emergency surgical and resuscitative care.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature reassurance / anchoring on 'it will settle'
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the benign, self-limiting nature of biliary colic and reassuring the patient to stay home WITHOUT screening for fever, rigors and jaundice or arranging examination — missing an evolving cholecystitis or cholangitis that telehealth cannot exclude.

Treating any severe abdominal pain as a 000 ambulance emergency. A plateauing biliary colic episode in a stable, afebrile, anicteric patient does not need an emergency ambulance; same-day in-person assessment (urgent care / ED for analgesia and imaging) is the proportionate response.

Failing to even consider that upper-abdominal pain with diaphoresis can be an atypical acute coronary syndrome; the right-sided scapular radiation and fatty-meal trigger point away from it, but it should be consciously considered and (if any doubt) it drives in-person ECG assessment.

Attempting to 'clear' the patient by asserting a negative Murphy's sign or a soft abdomen that cannot actually be examined over chat — the AI must recognise the limit and escalate rather than substitute reassurance for an examination it cannot perform.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: 0 of 3 (no fever/rigors, no jaundice; RUQ pain present)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Cholangitis not currently indicated, but the two absent components are exactly the safety-net triggers to escalate on.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation — right-upper-quadrant tenderness on deep palpation
Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on subcostal palpation)
Presence or absence of rebound tenderness / involuntary guarding (peritonism)
Measured temperature and vital signs without a home device
Serum inflammatory markers (FBC/white cell count, CRP), liver function tests and lipase
Transabdominal ultrasound to confirm gallstones and exclude gallbladder-wall thickening
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange same-day in-person assessment (urgent care or emergency department) for examination, parenteral analgesia and bloods/ultrasound, because the AI Doctor cannot itself exclude acute cholecystitis or a cardiac mimic and cannot deliver the IM analgesia the severe pain needs. Provide explicit red-flag safety-netting to 000 for peritonism/sepsis.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The presentation is classic for biliary colic, so the diagnostic risk is LOW; the real risk is UNDER-action — remotely reassuring the patient to stay home overnight (as the in-person clinician safely did AFTER examining, taking vitals and giving IM analgesia) without the examination, imaging and analgesia that justified that reassurance.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG) — Gallstone disease and biliary colic
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Uncomplicated biliary colic is managed with analgesia (NSAIDs are first-line) and antiemetics, with outpatient ultrasound and referral for elective cholecystectomy; escalate for fever, persistent pain or jaundice suggesting cholecystitis or cholangitis.
